title: Your Lead Scoring Model (1-10 Fit + Signal + Friction)

questions.question: What is the formula for the 1-10 lead scoring model?
questions.explanation: The scoring formula is FIT (0-4 points for ICP match) + SIGNAL (0-4 points for buying intent) - FRICTION (0-2 points for barriers to closing). This creates a 1-10 scale with clear action thresholds: 8-10 = immediate personal outreach, 5-7 = automated sequence, 1-4 = nurture or disqualify.

questions.question: A prospect who changed jobs within the past 90 days is how much more likely to buy than a static contact?

options.text: No difference — job changes don't affect buying behavior

questions.explanation: Prospects who changed jobs in the past 90 days are 3x more likely to buy. They're building new systems, proving themselves in new roles, and have fresh budget authority — making job changes one of the strongest intent signals you can track.

questions.question: Why does the model subtract FRICTION points instead of just lowering the FIT score?

options.text: Friction is the same as poor fit, just measured differently
Because friction is different from fit — a prospect can be a perfect ICP match with strong intent but still be hard to close due to industry or buying process
Friction only applies to enterprise deals

questions.explanation: Friction measures barriers to closing that are separate from ICP fit. A prospect might be a perfect match (4/4 FIT) with strong intent (3/4 SIGNAL), but if they're in healthcare with a 9-month procurement process, that friction factor needs to be captured independently to inform your outreach strategy.

questions.question: Lead scoring should be done once when a prospect is added and never updated afterward.
questions.explanation: False. Static scores go stale in weeks. Dynamic scoring uses automated triggers (job change alerts, email engagement, funding announcements, pricing page visits) to update scores in real-time. A lead who was a 5 last month can become a 9 this week when they change jobs and visit your pricing page.

questions.question: If you have 200 prospects, approximately how much time does lead scoring save compared to treating everyone equally?

options.text: No time savings — scoring adds overhead
About 26-30 hours saved (from 40+ hours down to 8-14 hours)

questions.explanation: Without scoring, 200 leads at equal treatment takes 40+ hours. With scoring: 20-40 hot leads (5-10 hours of personal outreach) + 100-120 warm leads (2-3 hours of sequence setup) + 40-80 cold leads (30 minutes to nurture/archive) = 8-14 hours total, saving approximately 26-30 hours.

questions.question: Define your personal FIT criteria (4 factors) based on your actual ICP. For each factor, explain what earns a point and give a concrete example of a prospect who would score 4/4 versus one who would score 1/4.
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response defines all four FIT factors (industry, title/role, company size, tech stack) with specific criteria for each. Look for concrete examples that demonstrate understanding of ICP matching and realistic differentiation between high-fit and low-fit prospects.
